---
engine: julia
---

## Regional Extreme Value Analysis ✏️ {.unnumbered}

This notebook demonstrates Bayesian hierarchical models for regional extreme value analysis.
We compare different levels of pooling—from no pooling (independent site analysis) to full pooling (complete data combination) to partial pooling (hierarchical models)—and show how borrowing strength across sites can improve parameter estimation.

```{julia}
using DataFrames
using Statistics
using Random
using Distributions
using Turing
using DynamicHMC
using CairoMakie
using LaTeXStrings
using Printf
using TidierData
using Dates
using Unitful
using ColorSchemes

CairoMakie.activate!(; type = "svg")
```

## Load real precipitation data

Instead of simulating data, let's load real annual maximum daily rainfall from Texas gauges:

```{julia}
#| code-fold: true
#| label: data-loading-functions
function parse_station_parts(
	station_number::Int, id, name, state, lat_str, lon_str, _elevation, years_of_data::Int = 0,
)
	return (
		stnid = station_number,
		noaa_id = String(strip(id)),
		name = String(strip(name)),
		state = String(strip(state)),
		latitude = parse(Float64, strip(lat_str)),
		longitude = parse(Float64, strip(lon_str)),
		years_of_data = years_of_data,
	)
end
function parse_station_parts(
	station_number::Int,
	id,
	name,
	city,
	state,
	lat_str,
	lon_str,
	_elevation,
	years_of_data::Int = 0,
)
	# Combine name and city for the full name
	full_name = "$(strip(name)), $(strip(city))"
	return (
		stnid = station_number,
		noaa_id = String(strip(id)),
		name = String(full_name),
		state = String(strip(state)),
		latitude = parse(Float64, strip(lat_str)),
		longitude = parse(Float64, strip(lon_str)),
		years_of_data = years_of_data,
	)
end
function parse_station_header(
	header_line::AbstractString, station_number::Int, years_of_data::Int = 0,
)
	# Split by commas and strip whitespace
	parts = [strip(p) for p in split(header_line, ",")]

	# Use splatting with method overloading
	return parse_station_parts(station_number, parts..., years_of_data)
end
function parse_rainfall_data(
	data_lines::Vector{<:AbstractString}, rainfall_unit, stnid::Int,
)
	# Pre-allocate vectors for better performance
	dates = Date[]
	years = Int[]
	rainfall = Float64[]
	stnids = Int[]

	# Parse available data with error handling
	for line in data_lines
		line = strip(line)
		if !isempty(line)
			parts = split(line)
			if length(parts) >= 2
				try
					date_str = parts[1]
					rain_str = parts[2]

					# Parse year directly for speed
					year_val = parse(Int, split(date_str, "/")[3])
					date = Date(date_str, "mm/dd/yyyy")
					rain_val = parse(Float64, rain_str)

					push!(dates, date)
					push!(years, year_val)
					push!(rainfall, rain_val)
					push!(stnids, stnid)
				catch e
					@warn "Error parsing line for station $stnid: $line"
					continue
				end
			end
		end
	end

	# If no data, return empty DataFrame
	if isempty(years)
		return DataFrame(;
			stnid = Int[], date = Date[], year = Int[], rainfall = typeof(1.0 * rainfall_unit)[],
		)
	end

	# Create complete year sequence and fill missing years
	min_year, max_year = extrema(years)
	complete_years = collect(min_year:max_year)

	# Create vectors for complete time series
	complete_stnids = Int[]
	complete_dates = Date[]
	complete_years_vec = Int[]
	complete_rainfall = Union{Float64, Missing}[]

	for yr in complete_years
		push!(complete_stnids, stnid)
		push!(complete_years_vec, yr)

		# Find if this year has data
		year_idx = findfirst(==(yr), years)
		if year_idx !== nothing
			push!(complete_dates, dates[year_idx])
			push!(complete_rainfall, rainfall[year_idx])
		else
			# Missing year - use January 1st as placeholder date
			push!(complete_dates, Date(yr, 1, 1))
			push!(complete_rainfall, missing)
		end
	end

	return DataFrame(;
		stnid = complete_stnids,
		date = complete_dates,
		year = complete_years_vec,
		rainfall = complete_rainfall .* rainfall_unit,
	)
end
function read_noaa_data(filename::String)
	# Read the data file
	txt = read(filename, String)
	lines = split(txt, '\n')

	# (1) Extract header info and set units  # <1>
	_header = lines[1]  # <2>
	rainfall_unit = u"inch"  # <3>

	remaining_content = join(lines[2:end], '\n')

	# (2) Split by blank lines which separate each gauge
	station_blocks = filter(!isempty, split(remaining_content, r"\n\s*\n"))

	stations = []
	all_rainfall_data = DataFrame[]

	# (3) For each gauge
	for (i, block) in enumerate(station_blocks)
		block_lines = split(strip(block), '\n')

		if !isempty(block_lines)
			# (a) Pull out the header row into station information
			header_line = block_lines[1]

			# (b) Parse the rainfall data with station ID
			data_lines = block_lines[2:end]
			rainfall_df = parse_rainfall_data(data_lines, rainfall_unit, i)

			# Count only non-missing years
			non_missing_years = sum(.!ismissing.(rainfall_df.rainfall))

			# Create station with years_of_data (non-missing only)
			station = parse_station_header(header_line, i, non_missing_years)
			push!(stations, station)
			push!(all_rainfall_data, rainfall_df)
		end
	end

	# Convert stations vector to DataFrame
	stations_df = DataFrame(stations)

	# Combine all rainfall data into single DataFrame
	rainfall_data_df = vcat(all_rainfall_data...)

	return stations_df, rainfall_data_df
end

# Load the data
stations_df, rainfall_df = read_noaa_data(joinpath(dirname(@__FILE__), "data", "dur01d_ams_na14v11.txt"))

first(stations_df, 5)
```

```{julia}
# Pick an Austin area station by noaa_id
austin_noaa_id = "41-0420"
austin_station = @filter(stations_df, noaa_id == !!austin_noaa_id)
austin_stnid = austin_station.stnid[1]
austin_rainfall = @filter(rainfall_df, stnid == !!austin_stnid)

let
	fig = Figure(size = (700, 400))
	ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
		title = "Annual Maximum Daily Rainfall at $(austin_station.name[1])",
		ylabel = "Rainfall (inches)")
	lines!(ax, austin_rainfall.year, ustrip.(u"inch", austin_rainfall.rainfall),
		color = :blue)
	fig
end
```

Now let's find other stations near Austin for regional analysis.
Specifically, we'll look for the ten closest stations with at least 50 years of data.
There are other, more formal, ways to define a region.

```{julia}
function calc_distance(lon1, lat1, lon2, lat2)
	# Haversine formula for distance between two points on Earth
	R = 6371.0  # Earth radius in km

	# Convert degrees to radians
	φ1, φ2 = deg2rad(lat1), deg2rad(lat2)
	Δφ = deg2rad(lat2 - lat1)
	Δλ = deg2rad(lon2 - lon1)

	a = sin(Δφ / 2)^2 + cos(φ1) * cos(φ2) * sin(Δλ / 2)^2
	c = 2 * atan(sqrt(a), sqrt(1 - a))

	return R * c
end

# Austin coordinates
austin_lat = austin_station.latitude[1]
austin_lon = austin_station.longitude[1]

# Find nearby stations with good data coverage
nearby_stations = @chain stations_df begin
	@filter(years_of_data >= 50)  # Use the years_of_data from station metadata
	@mutate(distance_km = calc_distance(!!austin_lon, !!austin_lat, longitude, latitude))
	@arrange(distance_km)
	first(10)
end
```

```{julia}
nearby_stnids = nearby_stations.stnid
nearby_rainfall = @chain rainfall_df begin
	@filter(stnid in !!nearby_stnids)
	@filter(year >= 1950)  # Focus on more recent data
end

let
	fig = Figure(size = (700, 400))
	ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
		title = "Annual Maximum Daily Rainfall at Nearby Stations",
		xlabel = "Year",
		ylabel = "Rainfall (inches)",
	)
	colors = ColorScheme(get(ColorSchemes.inferno, range(0.0, 1.0, length = length(nearby_stnids))))
	for (color, stn_id) in zip(colors, nearby_stnids)
		station_name = filter(row -> row.stnid == stn_id, nearby_stations).name[1]
		station_data = filter(row -> row.stnid == stn_id, nearby_rainfall)
		rainfall_in = ustrip.(u"inch", station_data.rainfall)
		lines!(ax, station_data.year, rainfall_in,
			color = color, label = "$station_name")
	end
	#axislegend(ax, position=:lt)
	fig
end
```

```{julia}
# Create regional dataset for analysis
selected_station_ids = nearby_stations.stnid
regional_data = @chain rainfall_df begin
	@filter(stnid in selected_station_ids)
	@filter(!ismissing(rainfall))  # Remove missing values
	@left_join(nearby_stations, stnid)
	@mutate(site = match(stnid, selected_station_ids))
	@mutate(annual_max = ustrip(rainfall))  # Remove units for modeling
	@select(site, year, annual_max, stnid, name, latitude, longitude)
end

function match(x, vec)
	return findfirst(==(x), vec)
end

# Apply the site numbering properly
for i in 1:nrow(regional_data)
	regional_data.site[i] = findfirst(==(regional_data.stnid[i]), selected_station_ids)
end

n_sites = length(selected_station_ids)
println("Using data from $n_sites stations near Austin")
println("Station details:")
@chain regional_data begin
	@group_by(site, stnid, name, latitude, longitude)
	@summarize(n_years = length(year), min_year = minimum(year), max_year = maximum(year))
	@arrange(site)
end
```

```{julia}
#| label: plot-real-data
#| fig-cap: "Annual maximum daily rainfall data from five Texas gauges showing regional variation within the state."

let
	fig = Figure(size = (800, 600))
	ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
		title = "Regional Annual Maximum Daily Rainfall Data (Texas)",
		xlabel = "Year",
		ylabel = "Annual Maximum (inches)")

	colors = ColorSchemes.okabe_ito.colors
	stnids = unique(regional_data.stnid)

	for (site, color) in zip(1:n_sites, colors)
		site_data = filter(row -> row.site == site, regional_data)
		scatter!(ax, site_data.year, site_data.annual_max,
			color = color, label = "Site $site", alpha = 0.7)
	end

	axislegend(ax, position = :rt)
	fig
end
```

## Generate regional data

Alternatively, we can still simulate data for comparison or when real data isn't available:

```{julia}
#| label: generate-regional-data

function generate_regional_data(n_sites, n_years; regional_params = nothing)
	Random.seed!(42)

	if regional_params === nothing
		# Default regional characteristics
		regional_params = (
			μ_mean = 80.0,    # Regional average location
			μ_sd = 15.0,      # Site-to-site variation in location
			σ_mean = 20.0,    # Regional scale parameter
			σ_sd = 5.0,       # Site-to-site variation in scale
			ξ = 0.05,          # Common shape parameter
		)
	end

	sites_data = DataFrame()

	for site in 1:n_sites
		# Site-specific parameters drawn from regional distributions
		site_μ = rand(Normal(regional_params.μ_mean, regional_params.μ_sd))
		site_σ = rand(LogNormal(log(regional_params.σ_mean), regional_params.σ_sd / regional_params.σ_mean))

		# Generate annual maxima for this site
		annual_max = rand(GeneralizedExtremeValue(site_μ, site_σ, regional_params.ξ), n_years)

		# Create site data
		site_df = DataFrame(
			site = fill(site, n_years),
			year = 1:n_years,
			annual_max = annual_max,
			true_μ = fill(site_μ, n_years),
			true_σ = fill(site_σ, n_years),
			true_ξ = fill(regional_params.ξ, n_years),
		)

		sites_data = vcat(sites_data, site_df)
	end

	return sites_data, regional_params
end

# Generate data for 5 sites with 30 years each
n_sites = 5
n_years = 30
regional_data, true_regional_params = generate_regional_data(n_sites, n_years)

println("Generated data for $n_sites sites with $n_years years each")
println("True regional parameters:")
for (key, val) in pairs(true_regional_params)
	println("  $key: $(round(val, digits=2))")
end

first(regional_data, 10)
```

```{julia}
#| label: plot-regional-data
#| fig-cap: "Annual maximum precipitation data for five sites showing site-to-site variation within a climatically similar region."

fig = Figure(size = (800, 600))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
	title = "Regional Annual Maximum Precipitation Data",
	xlabel = "Year",
	ylabel = "Annual Maximum (mm)")

colors = [:blue, :red, :green, :orange, :purple]

for site in 1:n_sites
	site_data = filter(row -> row.site == site, regional_data)
	scatter!(ax, site_data.year, site_data.annual_max,
		color = colors[site], label = "Site $site", alpha = 0.7)

	# Add true location parameter as horizontal line
	true_μ = site_data.true_μ[1]
	hlines!(ax, [true_μ], color = colors[site], linestyle = :dash, alpha = 0.5)
end

axislegend(ax, position = :rt)
fig
```

## No pooling: Independent analysis

First, let's analyze each site independently (no pooling):

```{julia}
#| label: no-pooling-model

@model function gev_no_pooling(y, site_ids)
	n_sites = maximum(site_ids)

	# Independent parameters for each site
	μ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)
	σ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)
	ξ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)

	for i in 1:n_sites
		μ[i] ~ Normal(80, 30)
		σ[i] ~ truncated(Normal(20, 10), 0, Inf)
		ξ[i] ~ Normal(0, 0.3)
	end

	# Likelihood
	for i in eachindex(y)
		site = site_ids[i]
		y[i] ~ GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ[site], σ[site], ξ[site])
	end

	return μ, σ, ξ
end

# Fit no pooling model
no_pool_chain = let
	model = gev_no_pooling(regional_data.annual_max, regional_data.site)
	sampler = externalsampler(DynamicHMC.NUTS())
	sample(model, sampler, 500; drop_warmup = true)
end

println("No pooling model fitted successfully")
println("Effective sample sizes:")
display(summarystats(no_pool_chain))
```

## Full pooling: Combined analysis

Now let's treat all data as coming from a single population (full pooling):

```{julia}
#| label: full-pooling-model

@model function gev_full_pooling(y)
	# Single set of parameters for all sites
	μ ~ Normal(80, 30)
	σ ~ truncated(Normal(20, 10), 0, Inf)
	ξ ~ Normal(0, 0.3)

	# Likelihood
	for i in eachindex(y)
		y[i] ~ GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ, σ, ξ)
	end
end

# Fit full pooling model
full_pool_chain = let
	model = gev_full_pooling(regional_data.annual_max)
	sampler = externalsampler(DynamicHMC.NUTS())
	sample(model, sampler, 500; drop_warmup = true)
end

println("Full pooling model fitted successfully")
display(summarystats(full_pool_chain))
```

## Partial pooling: Hierarchical model

The most sophisticated approach uses a hierarchical model with partial pooling:

```{julia}
#| label: hierarchical-model

@model function gev_hierarchical(y, site_ids)
	n_sites = maximum(site_ids)

	# Hyperparameters (regional population parameters)
	μ_region ~ Normal(80, 30)
	σ_region ~ truncated(Normal(20, 10), 0, Inf)
	ξ_region ~ Normal(0, 0.3)

	# Hyperparameters for site-to-site variation
	τ_μ ~ truncated(Normal(15, 10), 0, Inf)  # SD of site μ values
	τ_σ ~ truncated(Normal(5, 5), 0, Inf)    # SD of site σ values
	τ_ξ ~ truncated(Normal(0.1, 0.1), 0, Inf) # SD of site ξ values

	# Site-specific parameters drawn from regional distributions
	μ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)
	σ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)
	ξ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)

	for i in 1:n_sites
		μ[i] ~ Normal(μ_region, τ_μ)
		σ[i] ~ truncated(Normal(σ_region, τ_σ), 0, Inf)
		ξ[i] ~ Normal(ξ_region, τ_ξ)
	end

	# Likelihood
	for i in eachindex(y)
		site = site_ids[i]
		y[i] ~ GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ[site], σ[site], ξ[site])
	end

	return μ, σ, ξ, μ_region, σ_region, ξ_region, τ_μ, τ_σ, τ_ξ
end

# Fit hierarchical model
hierarchical_chain = let
	model = gev_hierarchical(regional_data.annual_max, regional_data.site)
	sampler = externalsampler(DynamicHMC.NUTS())
	sample(model, sampler, 500; drop_warmup = true)
end

println("Hierarchical model fitted successfully")
display(summarystats(hierarchical_chain))
```

## Simpler partial pooling: Shared shape parameter

A practical compromise is to share only the shape parameter (which is hardest to estimate) while allowing site-specific location and scale parameters:

```{julia}
#| label: partial-pooling-model

@model function gev_partial_pooling(y, site_ids)
	n_sites = maximum(site_ids)

	# Site-specific location and scale parameters
	μ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)
	σ = Vector{Real}(undef, n_sites)

	for i in 1:n_sites
		μ[i] ~ Normal(80, 30)
		σ[i] ~ truncated(Normal(20, 10), 0, Inf)
	end

	# Shared shape parameter across all sites
	ξ ~ Normal(0, 0.3)

	# Likelihood
	for i in eachindex(y)
		site = site_ids[i]
		y[i] ~ GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ[site], σ[site], ξ)
	end

	return μ, σ, ξ
end

# Fit partial pooling model
partial_pool_chain = let
	model = gev_partial_pooling(regional_data.annual_max, regional_data.site)
	sampler = externalsampler(DynamicHMC.NUTS())
	sample(model, sampler, 500; drop_warmup = true)
end

println("Partial pooling model fitted successfully")
display(summarystats(partial_pool_chain))
```

## Model comparison

Let's compare the performance of different pooling approaches:

```{julia}
#| label: parameter-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of parameter estimates across different pooling approaches. The hierarchical model provides a balance between site-specific estimates and regional information."

# Extract parameter estimates
function extract_site_params(chain, param_name, n_sites)
	if param_name in keys(chain)
		# Single parameter (full pooling)
		return fill(mean(chain[param_name]), n_sites)
	else
		# Site-specific parameters
		return [mean(chain[Symbol("$(param_name)[$i]")]) for i in 1:n_sites]
	end
end

# Get true values for comparison
true_site_params = DataFrame()
for site in 1:n_sites
	site_data = filter(row -> row.site == site, regional_data)
	push!(true_site_params, (
		site = site,
		true_μ = site_data.true_μ[1],
		true_σ = site_data.true_σ[1],
		true_ξ = site_data.true_ξ[1],
	))
end

# Extract estimates from each model
models = ["No Pooling", "Full Pooling", "Partial Pooling", "Hierarchical"]
chains = [no_pool_chain, full_pool_chain, partial_pool_chain, hierarchical_chain]

fig = Figure(size = (1200, 800))

# Location parameter comparison
ax1 = Axis(fig[1, 1],
	title = "Location Parameter μ",
	xlabel = "Site",
	ylabel = "μ (mm)")

x_pos = 1:n_sites
width = 0.2

for (i, (model_name, chain)) in enumerate(zip(models, chains))
	μ_estimates = extract_site_params(chain, :μ, n_sites)
	barplot!(ax1, x_pos .+ (i - 2.5) * width, μ_estimates,
		width = width, label = model_name, alpha = 0.7)
end

# Add true values
scatter!(ax1, x_pos, true_site_params.true_μ,
	color = :red, markersize = 10, marker = :star, label = "True")

ax1.xticks = (1:n_sites, string.(1:n_sites))
axislegend(ax1, position = :rt)

# Scale parameter comparison
ax2 = Axis(fig[1, 2],
	title = "Scale Parameter σ",
	xlabel = "Site",
	ylabel = "σ (mm)")

for (i, (model_name, chain)) in enumerate(zip(models, chains))
	σ_estimates = extract_site_params(chain, :σ, n_sites)
	barplot!(ax2, x_pos .+ (i - 2.5) * width, σ_estimates,
		width = width, alpha = 0.7)
end

scatter!(ax2, x_pos, true_site_params.true_σ,
	color = :red, markersize = 10, marker = :star)

ax2.xticks = (1:n_sites, string.(1:n_sites))

# Shape parameter comparison
ax3 = Axis(fig[2, 1:2],
	title = "Shape Parameter ξ",
	xlabel = "Site",
	ylabel = "ξ")

for (i, (model_name, chain)) in enumerate(zip(models, chains))
	ξ_estimates = extract_site_params(chain, :ξ, n_sites)
	barplot!(ax3, x_pos .+ (i - 2.5) * width, ξ_estimates,
		width = width, alpha = 0.7)
end

scatter!(ax3, x_pos, true_site_params.true_ξ,
	color = :red, markersize = 10, marker = :star)

ax3.xticks = (1:n_sites, string.(1:n_sites))

fig
```

## Return level comparison

The practical impact of different pooling approaches becomes clear when we examine return level estimates:

```{julia}
#| label: return-level-comparison
#| fig-cap: "100-year return level estimates showing how pooling affects extrapolation. The hierarchical model provides more stable estimates while preserving site-specific information."

# Function to calculate return levels with uncertainty
function calculate_return_levels(chain, return_period, site_id = nothing)
	if site_id === nothing
		# Full pooling case
		μ_samples = chain[:μ][:]
		σ_samples = chain[:σ][:]
		ξ_samples = chain[:ξ][:]
	else
		# Site-specific case
		μ_samples = chain[Symbol("μ[$site_id]")][:]
		if Symbol("σ[$site_id]") in keys(chain)
			σ_samples = chain[Symbol("σ[$site_id]")][:]
		else
			σ_samples = chain[:σ][:]  # Shared parameter
		end
		if Symbol("ξ[$site_id]") in keys(chain)
			ξ_samples = chain[Symbol("ξ[$site_id]")][:]
		else
			ξ_samples = chain[:ξ][:]  # Shared parameter
		end
	end

	# Calculate return levels for each sample
	p = 1 - 1 / return_period
	return_levels = similar(μ_samples)

	for i in eachindex(μ_samples)
		μ, σ, ξ = μ_samples[i], σ_samples[i], ξ_samples[i]
		if abs(ξ) < 1e-6
			return_levels[i] = μ - σ * log(-log(p))
		else
			return_levels[i] = μ + (σ / ξ) * ((-log(p))^(-ξ) - 1)
		end
	end

	return return_levels
end

# Calculate 100-year return levels for each site and model
return_period = 100
results = DataFrame()

for site in 1:n_sites
	for (model_name, chain) in zip(models, chains)
		if model_name == "Full Pooling"
			rl_samples = calculate_return_levels(chain, return_period)
		else
			rl_samples = calculate_return_levels(chain, return_period, site)
		end

		rl_mean = mean(rl_samples)
		rl_ci = quantile(rl_samples, [0.025, 0.975])

		push!(results, (
			site = site,
			model = model_name,
			return_level = rl_mean,
			ci_lower = rl_ci[1],
			ci_upper = rl_ci[2],
		))
	end

	# Add true return level
	site_data = filter(row -> row.site == site, regional_data)
	true_μ = site_data.true_μ[1]
	true_σ = site_data.true_σ[1]
	true_ξ = site_data.true_ξ[1]

	p = 1 - 1 / return_period
	if abs(true_ξ) < 1e-6
		true_rl = true_μ - true_σ * log(-log(p))
	else
		true_rl = true_μ + (true_σ / true_ξ) * ((-log(p))^(-true_ξ) - 1)
	end

	push!(results, (
		site = site,
		model = "True",
		return_level = true_rl,
		ci_lower = true_rl,
		ci_upper = true_rl,
	))
end

# Plot return level comparison
fig = Figure(size = (1000, 600))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
	title = "100-Year Return Level Estimates by Site and Model",
	xlabel = "Site",
	ylabel = "100-Year Return Level (mm)")

model_colors = Dict("No Pooling" => :blue, "Full Pooling" => :red,
	"Partial Pooling" => :green, "Hierarchical" => :orange,
	"True" => :black)

for model in ["No Pooling", "Full Pooling", "Partial Pooling", "Hierarchical", "True"]
	model_results = filter(row -> row.model == model, results)

	if model == "True"
		scatter!(ax, model_results.site, model_results.return_level,
			color = model_colors[model], markersize = 10, marker = :star,
			label = model)
	else
		# Plot point estimates with error bars
		errorbars!(ax, model_results.site .+ 0.1 * (findall(x -> x == model, models)[1] - 2.5),
			model_results.return_level,
			model_results.return_level .- model_results.ci_lower,
			model_results.ci_upper .- model_results.return_level,
			color = model_colors[model], linewidth = 2)

		scatter!(ax, model_results.site .+ 0.1 * (findall(x -> x == model, models)[1] - 2.5),
			model_results.return_level,
			color = model_colors[model], markersize = 8, label = model)
	end
end

ax.xticks = (1:n_sites, string.(1:n_sites))
axislegend(ax, position = :lt)
fig
```

## Shrinkage effect

A key feature of hierarchical models is shrinkage—site-specific estimates are "pulled" toward the regional mean. Let's visualize this effect:

```{julia}
#| label: shrinkage-visualization
#| fig-cap: "Shrinkage effect in hierarchical modeling. Site estimates are pulled toward the regional mean, with stronger shrinkage for sites with limited data or extreme values."

# Extract estimates
no_pool_μ = [mean(no_pool_chain[Symbol("μ[$i]")]) for i in 1:n_sites]
hierarchical_μ = [mean(hierarchical_chain[Symbol("μ[$i]")]) for i in 1:n_sites]
regional_μ = mean(hierarchical_chain[:μ_region])

fig = Figure(size = (800, 600))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
	title = "Shrinkage Effect: Location Parameter μ",
	xlabel = "No Pooling Estimate",
	ylabel = "Hierarchical Estimate")

# Diagonal line (no shrinkage)
xlims = (minimum([no_pool_μ; hierarchical_μ]) - 5,
	maximum([no_pool_μ; hierarchical_μ]) + 5)
ylims = xlims
plot_range = range(xlims[1], xlims[2], length = 100)
lines!(ax, plot_range, plot_range, color = :gray, linestyle = :dash,
	label = "No shrinkage")

# Regional mean lines
hlines!(ax, [regional_μ], color = :red, linestyle = :dot,
	label = "Regional mean")
vlines!(ax, [regional_μ], color = :red, linestyle = :dot)

# Site estimates
for i in 1:n_sites
	scatter!(ax, [no_pool_μ[i]], [hierarchical_μ[i]],
		color = colors[i], markersize = 12, label = "Site $i")

	# Arrow showing shrinkage direction
	if abs(no_pool_μ[i] - hierarchical_μ[i]) > 1
		arrows!(ax, [no_pool_μ[i]], [hierarchical_μ[i]],
			[hierarchical_μ[i] - no_pool_μ[i]], [0],
			color = colors[i], linewidth = 2, alpha = 0.7)
	end
end

xlims!(ax, xlims)
ylims!(ax, ylims)
axislegend(ax, position = :lt)
fig
```

## Model selection

Finally, let's compare models using information criteria:

```{julia}
#| label: model-selection

# For model comparison, we can use WAIC or LOO
# Here we'll compute a simple measure of fit: log predictive density

function log_predictive_density(chain, y, site_ids, model_type)
	n_samples = length(chain)
	log_density = 0.0

	for i in eachindex(y)
		site = site_ids[i]
		densities = Float64[]

		for j in 1:min(n_samples, 1000)  # Use subset for computational efficiency
			if model_type == "full_pooling"
				μ = chain[:μ][j]
				σ = chain[:σ][j]
				ξ = chain[:ξ][j]
			elseif model_type == "partial_pooling"
				μ = chain[Symbol("μ[$site]")][j]
				σ = chain[Symbol("σ[$site]")][j]
				ξ = chain[:ξ][j]
			else  # no_pooling or hierarchical
				μ = chain[Symbol("μ[$site]")][j]
				σ = chain[Symbol("σ[$site]")][j]
				ξ = chain[Symbol("ξ[$site]")][j]
			end

			try
				dist = GeneralizedExtremeValue(μ, σ, ξ)
				push!(densities, pdf(dist, y[i]))
			catch
				# Skip invalid parameter combinations
				continue
			end
		end

		if !isempty(densities)
			log_density += log(mean(densities))
		end
	end

	return log_density
end

# Calculate log predictive densities
model_fits = Dict(
	"No Pooling" => log_predictive_density(no_pool_chain, regional_data.annual_max,
		regional_data.site, "no_pooling"),
	"Full Pooling" => log_predictive_density(full_pool_chain, regional_data.annual_max,
		regional_data.site, "full_pooling"),
	"Partial Pooling" => log_predictive_density(partial_pool_chain, regional_data.annual_max,
		regional_data.site, "partial_pooling"),
	"Hierarchical" => log_predictive_density(hierarchical_chain, regional_data.annual_max,
		regional_data.site, "hierarchical"),
)

println("Model comparison (log predictive density, higher is better):")
for (model, lpd) in sort(collect(model_fits), by = x -> x[2], rev = true)
	println("  $model: $(round(lpd, digits=2))")
end
```

## Summary

This notebook demonstrates several key concepts in regional extreme value analysis:

### 1. Pooling spectrum
- **No pooling**: Maximum flexibility but high uncertainty, especially for sites with limited data
- **Full pooling**: Minimum uncertainty but ignores site-to-site differences
- **Partial pooling**: Compromises by sharing some parameters (often the shape parameter)
- **Hierarchical pooling**: Most sophisticated approach that balances site-specific and regional information

### 2. Shrinkage effect
Hierarchical models naturally shrink extreme site estimates toward the regional mean, providing more stable estimates while preserving important site-to-site differences.

### 3. Uncertainty reduction
Pooling information across sites reduces uncertainty in return level estimates, particularly for rare events where individual sites have limited information.

### 4. Model selection
The choice between pooling approaches should consider:
- Physical similarity between sites
- Amount of data available at each site
- Purpose of the analysis (site-specific vs. regional estimates)
- Model selection criteria

This framework extends the Bayesian principles from the probability and statistics chapter to spatial problems, showing how the same computational tools (`MCMC`, posterior prediction, model checking) apply to increasingly sophisticated model structures.
